audit_items.proof_trace: ber_strdup() at line 71 returns NULL on allocation failure (confirmed in ber_strdup_x: memory.c:643, p is returned as NULL when ber_memalloc_x fails). No NULL check exists before the result is used: ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(p) at line 80 is unconditionally called and immediately dereferences p in `for (ptr = s; *s != '\0'` (url.c:1625), causing a NULL pointer dereference. fopen(NULL, "rb") at line 82 also crashes. Every other allocation in the same function (e.g., ber_memrealloc at line 119) checks for NULL, confirming this is an oversight.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker floods the OpenLDAP server with large requests to induce memory exhaustion, then sends an LDAP modify/add operation containing an attribute value in LDIF URL format (`attribute:< file:///path`) that triggers ldif_parse_line → ldif_fetch_url → ldif_open_url; under OOM ber_strdup returns NULL, ldap_pvt_hex_unescape(NULL) dereferences it, crashing the slapd process (DoS).
